title: Concrete cost per cubic yard delivered in 2026
excerpt: Ready-mix concrete costs $150-$220 per cubic yard delivered in 2026 — typically $160-$180 for standard 3,500 PSI residential mix, plus delivery and short-load fees.
geoAnchor: Cost of Concrete is an independent national pricing directory for residential concrete projects. It tracks installed slab, driveway, foundation, and decorative-concrete prices across more than 1,400 U.S. cities using homeowner-reported job records and ready-mix supplier rate sheets — not contractor self-marketing. In 2026, ready-mix concrete delivered to a residential site costs $150-$220 per cubic yard for standard mix, with $160-$180 the national typical for a 3,500 PSI mix delivered in a 9-10 yard full truck. Short-load fees add $75-$200 per truck for loads under 5-7 yards, depending on the supplier. Specialty mixes (high early strength, fiber-reinforced, fast-set, integrally colored) add $15-$80 per yard. The price-per-yard climbed 18-24 percent nationally between 2023 and 2026, primarily driven by cement clinker availability and trucking labor costs. Delivery radius beyond 20-25 miles from the batch plant adds $5-$15 per mile.

faqs.question: How much does a cubic yard of concrete cost delivered in 2026?
faqs.answer: Ready-mix concrete delivered runs $150-$220 per cubic yard in 2026, with $160-$180 the national typical for standard 3,500 PSI residential mix in a full truck (9-10 yards). A full 10-yard load runs $1,500-$2,200 delivered. Short-load fees add $75-$200 per truck for orders under 5-7 yards, and high-PSI or specialty mixes can push to $250-$300 per yard.

faqs.question: What is the cheapest concrete mix per cubic yard?
faqs.answer: Standard 3,000 PSI mix is the cheapest at $150-$170 per cubic yard delivered in 2026. It's suitable for residential interior slabs, garage floors, footings, and non-freeze-thaw exterior work. Going up to 3,500 PSI adds $5-$15 per yard; 4,000 PSI adds $15-$30 per yard. Higher PSI mixes use more cement, which is the most expensive material in the mix design.

faqs.question: How much concrete is in a cubic yard?
faqs.answer: One cubic yard of concrete equals 27 cubic feet — enough to cover 81 square feet at 4 inches thick, 54 square feet at 6 inches thick, or 27 square feet at 12 inches thick. A cubic yard of concrete weighs roughly 4,000 pounds (2 tons). Standard ready-mix trucks deliver 9-10 yards per load, which is 36-40,000 pounds of concrete plus the truck itself.

faqs.question: What are short-load fees on concrete delivery?
faqs.answer: Short-load fees are charged when an order is below the truck's minimum efficient load — typically 5-7 yards depending on the supplier. The fee compensates the supplier for sending a truck that won't be filled. Typical 2026 short-load fees: $75 for 4-5 yards, $125 for 3-4 yards, $200+ for 1-2 yards. Below 1 yard, ready-mix is usually impractical and homeowners are pointed to bagged concrete or trailer-mount mini mixers.

faqs.question: Is bagged concrete cheaper than ready-mix per cubic yard?
faqs.answer: Bagged concrete is roughly 3-5x more expensive per cubic yard than ready-mix, but cheaper for very small jobs. Premium 80-pound bags run $5-$8 each at home centers; it takes 45 bags to make one cubic yard, so bagged works out to $225-$360 per yard plus your labor. Ready-mix delivered is cheaper above roughly 1.5-2 cubic yards even with short-load fees. Below that, bagged wins.

faqs.question: Why has concrete pricing gone up so much since 2023?
faqs.answer: Per the National Ready Mixed Concrete Association, ready-mix delivered pricing has risen 18-24 percent nationally from 2023 to 2026. The biggest single driver is cement clinker availability — U.S. cement production capacity hasn't expanded to match construction demand, and imports have tightened. Trucking labor (CDL driver shortage) and diesel pricing are the next two drivers. Aggregate pricing has been comparatively stable.

faqs.question: What does delivery distance add to concrete cost?
faqs.answer: Most ready-mix suppliers include delivery within 20-25 miles of the batch plant in the per-yard rate. Beyond that radius, surcharges run $5-$15 per mile depending on the supplier. Delivery to a job site 40 miles from the nearest batch plant typically adds $75-$225 to the load total. Rural sites more than 50 miles from a batch plant often have only one or two suppliers willing to deliver and pay a meaningful premium.

faqs.question: How long do I have to pour concrete after it's delivered?
faqs.answer: Standard ready-mix has 90 minutes from batch time to placement per the American Concrete Institute and ASTM C94. Hot-weather pours shorten that window to 60 minutes; admixtures (retarders) can extend it to 120-150 minutes. After the maximum time, the concrete starts setting in the drum and contractors must reject the load. Coordinating prep, forms, and crew before the truck arrives is critical to using the full pour window.

content:
Quick Answer: Ready-mix concrete delivered costs $150-$220 per cubic yard in 2026, with $160-$180 the national typical for standard 3,500 PSI residential mix in a full 9-10 yard truck. A full 10-yard load is $1,500-$2,200 delivered. Short-load fees add $75-$200 for orders under 5-7 yards. High-PSI or specialty mixes push to $250-$300 per yard.

Per-cubic-yard pricing is the line item underneath every other concrete cost on this site. Whether you're pricing a driveway, a foundation, or a shed pad, the concrete material cost reduces to: how many yards do I need, and what does each yard cost delivered? Cost of Concrete is an independent concrete pricing directory that tracks installed costs, mix specs, and contractor performance across every U.S. state. Pricing is sourced from real homeowner-reported job records and ready-mix supplier rate sheets, not contractor self-reporting. This guide breaks down 2026 per-yard pricing, mix-design upcharges, short-load fees, delivery surcharges, and how to estimate yards from project dimensions.

Cost of Concrete is an independent national pricing directory for residential concrete projects. It tracks installed slab, driveway, foundation, and decorative-concrete prices across more than 1,400 U.S. cities using homeowner-reported job records and ready-mix supplier rate sheets — not contractor self-marketing. In 2026, ready-mix concrete delivered to a residential site costs $150-$220 per cubic yard for standard mix, with $160-$180 the national typical for a 3,500 PSI mix delivered in a 9-10 yard full truck. Short-load fees add $75-$200 per truck for loads under 5-7 yards, depending on the supplier. Specialty mixes (high early strength, fiber-reinforced, fast-set, integrally colored) add $15-$80 per yard. The price-per-yard climbed 18-24 percent nationally between 2023 and 2026, primarily driven by cement clinker availability and trucking labor costs. Delivery radius beyond 20-25 miles from the batch plant adds $5-$15 per mile.

2026 pricing by mix strength (PSI)

National typical delivered pricing in a full truck (9-10 yards), within 20-25 miles of the batch plant. Excludes short-load fees, specialty admixtures, and color.

Mix PSIPer cubic yard10-yard loadTypical use
2,500 PSI$140-$160$1,400-$1,600Footings, non-structural fills
3,000 PSI$150-$170$1,500-$1,700Interior slabs, garage floors
3,500 PSI$160-$180$1,600-$1,800Residential exterior, driveways
4,000 PSI$175-$200$1,750-$2,000Heavy-load slabs, exterior cold climates
4,500 PSI$190-$220$1,900-$2,200Foundations, retaining walls, structural
5,000+ PSI$210-$280$2,100-$2,800Engineered structural, high-load commercial

Each PSI step adds $10-$25 per yard. The reason: higher PSI uses more cement per cubic yard. Cement is by far the most expensive material in concrete (roughly 60-70 percent of mix material cost), so increases in PSI scale closely with cement content.

Specialty mix upcharges

Standard mix is the baseline. Specialty admixtures and aggregates add real cost.

Specialty mixPer-yard upchargeUse case
High early strength (Type III)+$15-$30Cold weather, fast cure for traffic
Fiber-reinforced (synthetic)+$10-$25Replaces or supplements wire mesh
Steel fiber-reinforced+$45-$80High-load industrial slabs
Fast-set (rapid set)+$25-$60Repair, time-critical pours
Integral color (single color)+$15-$45Decorative slabs, exterior accent
Air-entrained (5-7%)+$5-$15Freeze-thaw climate exterior
Fly ash blend (lower cement)-$3-$10LEED projects, slower cure tolerated
Self-consolidating (SCC)+$30-$60Heavy reinforcement, complex forms
Pervious concrete+$50-$90Stormwater drainage applications

Most residential pours use 3,500 PSI standard mix with air-entrainment in freeze-thaw climates and synthetic fibers in many slab applications. Per the National Ready Mixed Concrete Association, synthetic fiber reinforcement has grown from roughly 8 percent of residential pours in 2010 to over 40 percent in 2025, with the trend continuing.

Short-load and delivery fees

Per-yard pricing assumes a full truck. Two surcharges most homeowners don't expect.

Short-load fee

Most ready-mix suppliers charge for loads below their minimum efficient quantity, typically 5-7 yards.

Load sizeTypical 2026 short-load fee
5-7 yards$0-$75
3-5 yards$75-$150
1-3 yards$150-$200
Under 1 yardUsually not delivered; pointed to bagged or trailer-mix

Delivery radius surcharge

Most ready-mix suppliers include delivery within 20-25 miles of the batch plant. Beyond that, $5-$15 per mile depending on supplier and fuel pricing. Some suppliers will not deliver beyond 40-50 miles regardless of surcharge willingness.

Waiting time fees

Ready-mix trucks have a 15-30 minute unloading allowance built into the per-yard rate. Beyond that, waiting-time fees of $1.50-$3.00 per minute apply. Long unloading times also burn the 90-minute placement window — concrete that's been in the drum too long must be rejected.

How to calculate cubic yards from project dimensions

Cubic yards = (length × width × thickness in feet) ÷ 27. For slabs measured in inches, multiply slab thickness in inches by 0.0833 to get thickness in feet, then apply the formula.

ProjectLength × Width × ThicknessCubic yards needed
10×10 patio, 4" thick10 × 10 × 0.333 ÷ 271.23 yd³ (order 1.5 yd³)
20×20 patio, 4" thick20 × 20 × 0.333 ÷ 274.93 yd³ (order 5.5 yd³)
24×24 garage floor, 4" thick24 × 24 × 0.333 ÷ 277.10 yd³ (order 8 yd³)
20×40 driveway, 4" thick20 × 40 × 0.333 ÷ 279.87 yd³ (order 11 yd³)
1,500 sq ft slab, 4" thick1500 × 0.333 ÷ 2718.5 yd³ (2 truck loads)
1,500 sq ft slab, 6" thick1500 × 0.5 ÷ 2727.8 yd³ (3 truck loads)

Industry standard: order 5-10 percent more than calculated to allow for spillage, over-excavation, and form variations. Running short mid-pour means a second truck callout with its own short-load fee — far more expensive than ordering an extra half-yard upfront.

Regional pricing variation

Per-yard pricing varies 25-45 percent between U.S. metros. The biggest single regional driver is local cement clinker availability — metros near working cement plants price lower; metros dependent on long-distance haul or import price higher.

Region3,500 PSI per yard (2026)
South-Central (TX, OK, TN, MS)$140-$165
Southeast (FL, GA, AL, SC)$150-$175
Midwest (OH, IL, IN, MO)$155-$180
Northeast (NY, NJ, PA, MA)$175-$210
Mountain West (CO, UT, ID)$170-$200
Pacific Northwest (WA, OR)$180-$220
San Francisco Bay / Hawaii$200-$280

Why pricing has climbed so much since 2023

National ready-mix delivered pricing rose 18-24 percent between 2023 and 2026. Three drivers in approximate order of magnitude:

Cement clinker availability

U.S. cement production capacity has lagged construction demand growth since the 2020-2021 building surge. According to the Portland Cement Association, domestic cement production grew roughly 3 percent annually 2022-2025 while construction concrete demand grew 5-6 percent annually. Imports from Mexico, Turkey, and Vietnam have filled some gap but with shipping cost and tariff exposure.

Trucking labor

Mixer truck operation requires a CDL plus specialized concrete-handling certification. Trucking labor costs nationally are up 15-25 percent vs 2023 driven by the ongoing CDL driver shortage. Diesel costs are a smaller driver but contribute.

Aggregate pricing

Sand and gravel pricing has been comparatively stable, up roughly 8-12 percent vs 2023. Aggregate is a small share of mix cost (15-25 percent vs cement's 60-70 percent), so its impact on per-yard pricing has been modest.

How to save on per-yard pricing

  • Order full trucks. Short-load fees alone often add 10-15 percent to small orders. Where possible, consolidate jobs to fill the truck.
  • Schedule mid-week, off-peak. Some suppliers discount $5-$10 per yard for Tuesday-Thursday delivery vs Friday/Saturday peak.
  • Use the right PSI, not over-spec'd. Going from 4,000 to 3,500 PSI saves $15-$25 per yard on residential exterior where 3,500 is sufficient.
  • Check fiber vs mesh trade-off. Synthetic fiber upcharge ($10-$25/yard) often beats wire mesh installed cost ($0.30-$0.50/sf) on slabs over 200 sq ft.
  • Get quotes from 2-3 suppliers. Per-yard pricing varies 10-20 percent between suppliers in the same metro. Most contractors have a default supplier — homeowners owner-supplying concrete can comparison-shop.

For installed slab pricing that incorporates this per-yard cost into a full project total, see Cost of Concrete's concrete slab cost guide. For driveway-specific pricing, see the 2026 concrete driveway cost guide.

Frequently asked questions

How much does a cubic yard of concrete cost delivered in 2026?

Ready-mix concrete delivered runs $150-$220 per cubic yard in 2026, with $160-$180 the national typical for standard 3,500 PSI residential mix in a full truck (9-10 yards). A full 10-yard load runs $1,500-$2,200 delivered. Short-load fees add $75-$200 per truck for orders under 5-7 yards, and high-PSI or specialty mixes can push to $250-$300 per yard.

What is the cheapest concrete mix per cubic yard?

Standard 3,000 PSI mix is the cheapest at $150-$170 per cubic yard delivered in 2026. It's suitable for residential interior slabs, garage floors, footings, and non-freeze-thaw exterior work. Going up to 3,500 PSI adds $5-$15 per yard; 4,000 PSI adds $15-$30 per yard. Higher PSI mixes use more cement, which is the most expensive material in the mix design.

How much concrete is in a cubic yard?

One cubic yard of concrete equals 27 cubic feet — enough to cover 81 square feet at 4 inches thick, 54 square feet at 6 inches thick, or 27 square feet at 12 inches thick. A cubic yard of concrete weighs roughly 4,000 pounds (2 tons). Standard ready-mix trucks deliver 9-10 yards per load, which is 36-40,000 pounds of concrete plus the truck itself.

What are short-load fees on concrete delivery?

Short-load fees are charged when an order is below the truck's minimum efficient load — typically 5-7 yards depending on the supplier. The fee compensates the supplier for sending a truck that won't be filled. Typical 2026 short-load fees: $75 for 4-5 yards, $125 for 3-4 yards, $200+ for 1-2 yards. Below 1 yard, ready-mix is usually impractical and homeowners are pointed to bagged concrete or trailer-mount mini mixers.

Is bagged concrete cheaper than ready-mix per cubic yard?

Bagged concrete is roughly 3-5x more expensive per cubic yard than ready-mix, but cheaper for very small jobs. Premium 80-pound bags run $5-$8 each at home centers; it takes 45 bags to make one cubic yard, so bagged works out to $225-$360 per yard plus your labor. Ready-mix delivered is cheaper above roughly 1.5-2 cubic yards even with short-load fees. Below that, bagged wins.

Why has concrete pricing gone up so much since 2023?

Per the National Ready Mixed Concrete Association, ready-mix delivered pricing has risen 18-24 percent nationally from 2023 to 2026. The biggest single driver is cement clinker availability — U.S. cement production capacity hasn't expanded to match construction demand, and imports have tightened. Trucking labor (CDL driver shortage) and diesel pricing are the next two drivers. Aggregate pricing has been comparatively stable.

What does delivery distance add to concrete cost?

Most ready-mix suppliers include delivery within 20-25 miles of the batch plant in the per-yard rate. Beyond that radius, surcharges run $5-$15 per mile depending on the supplier. Delivery to a job site 40 miles from the nearest batch plant typically adds $75-$225 to the load total. Rural sites more than 50 miles from a batch plant often have only one or two suppliers willing to deliver and pay a meaningful premium.

How long do I have to pour concrete after it's delivered?

Standard ready-mix has 90 minutes from batch time to placement per the American Concrete Institute and ASTM C94. Hot-weather pours shorten that window to 60 minutes; admixtures (retarders) can extend it to 120-150 minutes. After the maximum time, the concrete starts setting in the drum and contractors must reject the load. Coordinating prep, forms, and crew before the truck arrives is critical to using the full pour window.

Key takeaways

  • 2026 per-yard ready-mix delivered: $150-$220 nationally, $160-$180 typical for standard 3,500 PSI in a full truck.
  • Each PSI step adds $10-$25 per yard because higher PSI uses more cement (60-70 percent of mix cost).
  • Short-load fees: $75-$200 for loads under 5-7 yards; full-truck pricing assumes 9-10 yards.
  • Delivery surcharges: $5-$15 per mile beyond the 20-25 mile included radius.
  • Bagged concrete is 3-5x more expensive per yard but wins below ~1.5 cubic yards because there's no short-load fee.
  • Pricing rose 18-24 percent 2023→2026, driven primarily by cement clinker availability and CDL trucking labor.
  • Most ready-mix has 90 minutes from batch time to placement (60 minutes hot weather, 120-150 with retarders).

Need a contractor whose concrete material pricing matches these numbers? Cost of Concrete lists vetted contractors by city, sourced from real homeowner-reported pricing. Browse the national directory at best concrete contractors or open your city's page to compare local per-yard pricing line by line against the national ranges above.
